reasoning: Attacked by independent grep, as instructed. `grep -rn "returning-ace|ReturningAce|beginPass|evadeCheck|closesPast" src/` returns ONLY src/core/returning-ace.ts's own definitions plus one comment in src/core/enemy.ts:62. There is no import of the module anywhere in src/. main.ts's import block (26-52) is confirmed to contain camera, horizon, flight, enemy, waves, biplane, blimp, lives, landscape, scene, timing, guns, explosion, scoring, topology, events, audio, audio-dispatch and the @arcade/shared modules — and NOT returning-ace. The module is dead. The ROM hook is real and cited correctly: RBARON.MAC:2250-2252 is verbatim `BIT GMEND0 ;RETURNING PLANE` / `BPL 15$ ;NO` / `JMP NWENME ;ELSE - INITIALIZE`, and MAIN:788 is `JSR NWPLNE ;GENERATE MOTION OBJECTS`. The supporting ROM signals also exist and are not decoys: RBARON.MAC:128 `BEFLAG: .BLKB 1 ;PLANE BEHIND FLAG`, :171 `GMEND0: .BLKB 2 ;END LIFE FLAGS (D7=RETURN, D6=RP)`, :184 `ENSIDE: .BLKB 1 ;ENEMY PLANE X ENTER (EXIT) SIDE (D7)`. Radix is irrelevant here (no numeric claim). Held.
corrections: "MAIN calls NWPLNE every calc frame" is FALSE as written. RBARON.MAC:788 is reached only after FOUR gates all pass: GMEND1 EOL clear (:774-775), GAMODE freeze bit clear (:780-782), GREND clear (:783-784), and GRMODE ground-sequence clear (:786-787). NWPLNE is NOT called on an end-of-life frame, a frozen frame, a crashed frame, or a ground wave.

"ROM 625 NWPLNE passes, each able to re-enter the ace" is a gross overstatement. The 625 arithmetic is right (60 / 0.096 = 625 calc frames per minute) but it is a ceiling on calc frames, not on ace re-entries. Inside NWPLNE the BIT GMEND0 at :2250 is reached only when plane #0 is neither in flight nor exploding (`LDA PLSTAT+6 / AND I,0C0 / BNE 17$`, :2241-2243) AND the plane count DECs to zero (`DEC PLSTAT+7 / BNE 17$`, :2244-2245). The re-entry gate fires rarely, not 625x/min.

SCOPE — the cited lines are the RE-ENTRY HOOK, not the mechanic's driver. RBARON.MAC:2250-2252 only says 'if the return flag is armed, JMP NWENME (re-initialize the plane)'. The returning plane's ATTACK and KILL live in EOLSEQ (RBARON.MAC:1057-1098): `STA GMEND0 ;PLANES FLY AWAY` (:1069), `LDA ENSIDE ;CHECK FOR PLAYER DEATH` (:1081), `BIT BEFLAG` (:1088), `LDA GMEND0 ;EOL BY RETURNING PLANE` (:1095), and the arm/disarm at P.UPD0 (:2727-2730) and :2695 `STX GMEND0 ;CLR RETURNING (X=0)`. A wiring job driven ONLY off :2250-2252 will re-spawn the plane and still never kill the player. Note GMEND0 is declared as an END-LIFE flag byte (:171) — which means the returning ace's damage exits through GMEND1/EOL, i.e. exactly the channel CD-011 proves is a dead store. CD-010 and CD-011 are ONE fix, not two.

reasoning: Attacked by independent grep, as instructed. `grep -rn "lives|loseLife" src/` over main.ts yields exactly: :38 the import, :342 the declaration, :406 the write `lives = loseLife(lives).lives`, and three prose comments (:122, :368, :401). No read, no comparison, no render, no loop gate. It is a textbook dead store. Worse than the finding says: `loseLife` RETURNS `{lives, gameOver}` (src/core/lives.ts:119-122) and main.ts:406 discards `.gameOver` by destructuring only `.lives`; and `count` is floored by `Math.max(0, ...)` (lives.ts:120), so the blimp can indeed fire forever with no effect. The ROM citation is verbatim-correct: RBARON.MAC:774 `LDA GMEND1`, :775 `BMI 10$ ;END OF LIFE`, and 10$ IS at :789 `JSR PLMOTN ;UPDATE MOTION OBJECTS (PLANES & DRONES)`. Held.
corrections: The branch skips MORE than the finding enumerates. `BMI 10$` at :775 jumps over lines 776-788, i.e. past POTSCL (:778) and NEWSHL (:779) — as claimed — but ALSO past the GAMODE freeze test (:780-782), the GREND ground-crash test (:783-784), PFMOTN ;UPDATE PLAYFIELD (:785), the GRMODE test (:786-787) and NWPLNE (:788). A dead player's world does not move at all except PLMOTN. Any implementation must gate ALL of that, not just controls and guns.

"the death/respawn sequence then runs" is NOT supported by the cited lines. MAIN:774-789 only skips work; the death/respawn sequence is EOLSEQ (RBARON.MAC:1057+), which is called from elsewhere. The finding asserts it without a cite.

SCOPE is larger than the finding states: `tickGrace` and `enemiesDisabled` (src/core/lives.ts:126-132) are ALSO never called anywhere in src/ — so the WO.CNT post-death spawn grace is dead too, not just the counter. The whole of lives.ts is unreachable from the running game, not merely its count.

reasoning: Attacked the LSR decode specifically, as instructed. RBARON.MAC:926-927 is verbatim `INTWAIT:LSR CALFLG ;WAIT FOR FRAME TIME` / `BCC INTWAIT`. LSR is destructive and the claim's trace is arithmetically correct: CALFLG=2 (0b10) -> LSR yields 1, C = old bit0 = 0 -> BCC taken, loop; LSR yields 0, C = 1 -> exit. Backlog drained, MAIN proceeds to exactly ONE more motion update. Corroborated by the only other CALFLG writer: `INC CALFLG` at RBGRND.MAC:229 (there are exactly three CALFLG sites in the whole quarry — RBARON.MAC:81 the .BLKB, RBARON.MAC:926, RBGRND.MAC:229 — so nothing else overrides). Wall-clock consequence checked by hand: a 200 ms overrun leaves CALFLG=2; INTWAIT returns immediately; the next MAIN pass runs one step; the loop re-syncs at t=288 ms having advanced the sim TWO steps across THREE calc periods. The sim permanently loses a step — it never double-steps. Our side verified: main.ts:373 `while (accumulator >= SIM_TIMESTEP_S)` is a catch-up loop, SIM_TIMESTEP_S = 24/250 = 0.096 exactly (timing.ts:31). Held.
corrections: NUMBER IS WRONG: "0.25 / 0.096 = 2.6, so at most 2 steps run in one rAF" ignores the accumulator RESIDUAL. After the while loop, accumulator is in [0, 0.096). The next frame adds dt <= 0.25 (main.ts:356), so the accumulator can reach just under 0.096 + 0.25 = 0.346 s, and 0.346 / 0.096 = 3.6 -> THREE steps can run in one rAF, not two. Three steps require only a residual >= 0.038 s, which is common. Max catch-up burst is 3x, not 2x.

reasoning: Re-opened both sides. Our doc, docs/red-baron-1980-source-findings.md:113-114, reads verbatim: "...(watchdog only trips after `CALFLG >= 0x40` ~ 64 missed frames, `RBGRND.MAC:196-202`)". The cited lines say the opposite: RBGRND.MAC:196 `12$: LDA CALFLG ;CHK FRAME(S)`, :197 `CMP I,3`, :198 `BCS 15$ ;3*CALCULATION LOOP TIME W/O FRAME`, and 15$ at :202 is `JMP 15$ ;WAIT FOR WATCHDOG TO RESTART` — a deliberate infinite loop, with the pet at :204 `20$: STA A,WATCH ;CLR WATCHDOG`. Radix hunted: RBGRND.MAC has `.RADIX 16` at :6 and its only decimal island starts at :723, so line 197's `3` sits in a hex region — but 3 is 3 in either radix, so the doc's 0x40 cannot be a radix artefact. It is simply wrong. I also searched the whole quarry for any other watchdog counter that could justify 0x40: WATCH is only ever a STORE target (RBARON.MAC:519 `WATCH =1400`, plus stores in RBINT.MAC and RBGRND.MAC:204) — it is never compared against anything. There is no 0x40 threshold anywhere. 64/3 = 21.3, so the "21x" in the title is right. Killed the doc's number cleanly; the finding stands.
corrections: BRANCH POLARITY IS BACKWARDS: the finding says the pet at :204 is "reached only on the non-taken branch". It is reached on the TAKEN branch of `BCS 20$` (:201) — 20$ IS :204. The flow is: CALFLG >= 3 -> BCS 15$ taken -> hang; else stack check `CPX I,90` (:200), and SP >= 0x90 -> BCS 20$ TAKEN -> pet. Only the FALL-THROUGH of :201 (stack too low) drops into the 15$ hang. The finding's conclusion is unaffected; its description of the control flow is not.

TIMING NUANCE: the CALFLG check (:196-198) executes BEFORE the `INC CALFLG` (:229) within the same NMI, so the hang fires on the NMI AFTER CALFLG first reaches 3, not on the one that sets it. Immaterial in practice (4 ms), but "trips at CALFLG >= 3" should read "trips on the first NMI that READS CALFLG >= 3".

The inference "MAIN was expected to finish comfortably inside 96 ms" is stronger than the ROM supports. The ROM tolerates a 2-frame backlog (CALFLG 0/1/2 are all survivable; INTWAIT's LSR drains 2 -> 0), so MAIN could overrun by up to ~2 calc frames without a reset. The budget is tight, but it is not a single-frame hard deadline. The 21x correction to the doc is right; the accompanying rhetoric overshoots.

reasoning: An accusation of a citation error must itself be perfectly cited, so I re-opened every line on both sides. (1) doc:100 reads `| **Game-logic / calc frame (\`CALCNT\`)** | /24 NMIs | **96 ms / ~10.42 Hz** | \`RBARON.MAC:620\`; \`RBGRND.MAC:221-229\` |`. RBARON.MAC:620 is a lone tab (blank); `CALCNT\t=18` is on :621. Off-by-one CONFIRMED. It IS copied into src/core/timing.ts:18 (`/** CALCNT 0x18 — ... (RBARON.MAC:620). */`) and also into timing.ts:11's cite line. (2) doc:101 reads `| Calc frame, attract (\`BNRCNT\`) | /12 NMIs | 48 ms / ~20.83 Hz | \`RBARON.MAC:621\` |`. :621 is CALCNT; `BNRCNT\t=0C` is on :622. CONFIRMED. (3) doc:104 reads `| Player shell sub-stepping (\`SHLMOT\`) | **4x** per calc-frame | — | \`RBARON.MAC:5186-5198\` |`. RBARON.MAC:5186 is verbatim `CHKSM6:\t.BYTE 6C` — a checksum byte in a different .SBTTL. SHLMOT is at :5192 (its only definition; the only other reference is `JSR SHLMOT` at :814) and runs :5192-5204. CONFIRMED, and guns.ts:43 repeats the bad range verbatim. Line-number alignment between my reads and the finding's was independently anchored on two verbatim matches (CHKSM6 at 5186, BNRCNT at 622). All three citation errors are real. The table's VALUES (24, 12, 4x) are all correct — verified 4 PSTSHL calls at :5195, :5198, :5201, :5204.
corrections: THE FINDING'S OWN ARITHMETIC IS WRONG on point (3). The truncated range 5186-5198 contains PSTSHL at :5195 and :5198 — TWO sub-steps, not three. So it hides sub-steps 3 AND 4 (:5201 and :5204), and makes SHLMOT look like a **2x** sub-step, not a 3x. The finding says "hiding the 4th sub-step ... making it look like a 3x sub-step" — both counts are off by one. The danger it describes is real but worse than stated: a reader correcting SHELL_SUBSTEPS from the doc's range would set it to 2, not 3.

Point (1) understates the blast radius: the bad `RBARON.MAC:620` cite appears TWICE in timing.ts — at :11 (the file-header cite line) and at :18 (the CALC_FRAME_NMIS doc comment). Fixing only :18 leaves the error in the file header.

reasoning: The DIVERGENCE is real and every fact the finding asserts checks out — but its central identification is wrong, and the correction below is the load-bearing part of this verdict. Verified ROM: RBGRND.MAC:244 `JSR PLPROP` sits behind `BIT S.FLAG ;PROGRAM CALCULATING?` / `BPL 10$ ;NO` (:238-239), `LDA INTFLG ;EOF (VG) ?` / `BEQ 10$` (:240-241) and a VG-halt check (:242-243). Polarity decoded: MAIN:763 `DEC S.FLAG` sets it to 0FF (calculating, N=1) and INTWAIT:928 `INC S.FLAG` clears it to 0 — so the NMI services every VG end-of-frame that lands while MAIN is busy or spinning in INTWAIT, and MAIN:857 `JSR PLPROP ;SWITCH PLANE PROP` services the one frame after INTWAIT cleared the flag. Net: exactly once per VG frame = 62.5 Hz (FRMECNT=4, RBGRND.MAC:61, in that file's hex region). PLPROP's PROP.F half (RBARON.MAC:882-889) does INY/INY, `CPY I,6`, `LDY I,0` — offsets 0/2/4, a 3-phase cycle — and pokes the JSRL into VGRAM+0794/0795 or VGRAM+0F94/0F95 (:894-895, :899-900) per the double-buffer. 62.5/3 = 20.83 cycles/sec. Our side verified by grep: PROPS / DBPROP_POINTS / PPROPA-C appear ONLY in src/core/topology.ts (definitions) and src/tools/romCompare.ts (an offline ROM-diff tool). Zero references in main.ts, biplane.ts, or anywhere in src/shell/. The prop is advanced 0 times per second. Held.
corrections: WRONG PROP — this is the big one. PLPROP animates TWO propellers, and the finding decoded only the first, cutting the routine at :889. RBARON.MAC:198 declares `PROP.F: .BLKB 1 ;PLANE PROP PICTURE OFFSET` — the ENEMY PLANE's prop, which is why :883 `BMI 20$ ;NO PLANE PROP` skips it when no plane is up, and why RBARON.MAC:399 names its table `.PROPS = PROPS+6 ;PLANE PROP BUFFER ADDRS (VG)`. The PLAYER's cockpit prop is the SECOND half of PLPROP, :901-921: it advances `PLYPRP` (RBARON.MAC:197, `;PLAYER PROP OFFSET`) by INY/INY with `CPY I,0E` / `LDY I,0` — offsets 0,2,4,6,8,0A,0C, a SEVEN-phase cycle, not three — and patches the JSRL from `LNEPRP` (RBARON.MAC:408, `LNEPRP = DBPROP+13D ;PLAYER PROP PICTURES(JSRL TABLE)`; 037007.XXX:591 `LNEPRP: JSRL PROP0`) into VGRAM+012/013 or VGRAM+0812/0813, gated on GMEND1 (:907-908) and GAMODE&2 (:909-911, ';NOT WITH HST INFO').

CONSEQUENCE: the finding's headline rationale — that wiring PROPS into draw() is "the fidelity-correct way to make a 10.4 Hz cockpit stop reading as a slideshow" — is FALSE. PROPS is the ENEMY plane's prop; it is only on screen when an enemy plane is, and it does nothing for an empty sky. The thing that makes the COCKPIT feel alive is the player's prop (PLYPRP/LNEPRP), and its geometry is NOT in the repo: `grep -rn "LNEPRP|PROP0|PLYPRP" src/` returns NOTHING. So "everything needed is already in the repo and byte-checked" is only half true, and size:s is right only for the enemy prop — the player prop needs new geometry transcribed from 037007.XXX first.
TITLE IS WRONG: "the ONE thing the ROM animates BETWEEN calc frames" — it is TWO things (both props), both inside PLPROP.

RATE: 62.5/3 = 20.83 is the rate of the 3-frame ANIMATION CYCLE, not of a physical revolution. Nothing in the ROM establishes that one 3-picture cycle equals one 360-degree turn of a 2-blade prop. Call it 20.8 animation cycles/sec. The player prop's 7-phase cycle runs at 62.5/7 = 8.93 cycles/sec — a different rate the finding does not mention at all.

reasoning: Verified BNRCNT's value, its radix region, and that it actually reloads C.CNTR — all three, as instructed. VALUE: RBARON.MAC:622 is verbatim `BNRCNT\t=0C\t\t\t;BANNER DISPLAY CALCULATION COUNT`. RADIX: RBARON.MAC has `.RADIX 16` at :74 and its ONLY decimal island is :6217-6280 (`.RADIX 10` at 6217, back to `.RADIX 16` at 6281). Line 622 is far outside it, so 0C is HEX = 12 decimal. Arithmetic: 12 NMIs x 4 ms = 48 ms -> 20.833 Hz, exactly double the 24 x 4 ms = 96 ms / 10.417 Hz gameplay rate. RELOAD PATH: RBGRND.MAC:221-229 verbatim — `DEC C.CNTR ;CALCULATION FRAME CNTR` / `BNE 50$` / `LDX I,CALCNT` / `LDA GAMODE` / `AND I,BNRBIT` / `BEQ 35$` / `LDX I,BNRCNT` / `35$: STX C.CNTR` / `INC CALFLG`. So when (GAMODE & BNRBIT) is NONZERO the BEQ is not taken and X is overwritten with BNRCNT before the STX — C.CNTR genuinely reloads with 12, not 24. BNRBIT is RBARON.MAC:610 `BNRBIT =10 ;BANNER DISLAY BIT` (hex 10 = bit 4), also in the hex region. Our side verified exactly as stated: src/core/timing.ts exports precisely MASTER_NMI_HZ, CALC_FRAME_NMIS, DISPLAY_FRAME_NMIS, SIM_HZ, DISPLAY_HZ, SIM_TIMESTEP_S — no banner constant — and main.ts has no attract mode. NO_COUNTERPART / wont_fix is the right call. Held.
corrections: THE SUPPORTING ARGUMENT IS BOGUS, even though the conclusion is right. The finding claims BNRCNT "is spelled `0C` with the leading zero the ROM uses for hex literals" and that this is "the strongest independent proof that CALCNT is HEX". It proves nothing: MACRO-65 requires a leading digit on ANY literal beginning with A-F regardless of radix (the same file writes `0FF`, `0C0`, `0F0` in a hex region and would write `0C` identically in a decimal one). The finding's second argument — "the doc's own 20.83 Hz only works if 0C = 12" — is circular. The ACTUAL proof is the `.RADIX 16` directive at RBARON.MAC:74 with the file's only decimal island at :6217-6280; cite that, not the leading zero. If the hex reading of CALCNT is ever challenged, this finding as written does not defend it.
